name: transform
context: Transform, coerce, preprocess, and pipe data through Zod schema chains to reshape validated input
info: This skill covers Zod's data transformation capabilities: .transform() to map validated data into a new shape (the output type differs from the input type), .default() to provide fallback values for undefined input, .catch() to provide fallback values when validation fails, .preprocess() to modify raw input before validation, .pipe() to chain schemas where the output of one feeds into the next, z.coerce for automatic type coercion (string→number, string→date, etc.), .optional() / .nullable() / .nullish() for optionality, and .brand() for nominal typing. Transforms are essential for API response normalization, form data parsing, environment variable coercion, and building typed pipelines. When a schema has transforms, the input and output types differ — use z.input<> and z.output<> (or z.infer<> for output) to extract each. See [[zod/schemas]] for defining shapes and [[zod/validation]] for constraints.
triggers: coerce string to number in zod

sections.title: .transform() — reshape validated data
sections.body: `.transform(fn)` takes a function that receives the validated value and returns a new value. The output type becomes the schema's inferred type. Chain multiple `.transform()` calls for multi-step pipelines. Use async functions with `.parseAsync()`. Transforms run AFTER validation succeeds — if validation fails, the transform never executes. Use `z.input<typeof schema>` for the pre-transform type and `z.output<typeof schema>` (or `z.infer`) for the post-transform type.

sections.title: z.coerce — automatic type coercion
sections.body: `z.coerce.string()`, `z.coerce.number()`, `z.coerce.boolean()`, `z.coerce.date()`, `z.coerce.bigint()` pass raw input through the type's constructor before validation. `z.coerce.number()` calls `Number(input)`, so `"42"` becomes `42`. Essential for parsing environment variables, URL params, and form data where everything arrives as strings. Coerced schemas still support all validators: `z.coerce.number().int().positive()` coerces then validates.

sections.title: .pipe() — schema chaining
sections.body: `.pipe(nextSchema)` feeds the output of one schema as the input to another. Useful for coerce-then-validate patterns: `z.string().transform(Number).pipe(z.number().int())`. The first schema validates and optionally transforms, then the result is validated by the piped schema. Cleaner than chaining `.transform()` with runtime type checks.

sections.title: .default() and .catch() — defaults and fallbacks
sections.body: `.default(value)` substitutes a value when input is `undefined`. Accepts a static value or a function for dynamic defaults. `.catch(value)` substitutes a value when validation FAILS — the schema never throws. Combine for robust parsing: `z.coerce.number().default(0).catch(0)` handles both undefined and invalid input. `.default()` affects `z.input` (input becomes optional) while `.catch()` does not.

sections.title: .brand() and .readonly() — nominal types and immutability
sections.body: `.brand<T>()` adds a nominal type brand to the inferred type, creating opaque types that prevent accidental mixing of structurally identical values (e.g., UserId vs OrderId). Branded types only exist at the type level — no runtime overhead. `.readonly()` makes the inferred type deeply readonly. Both are type-level-only transforms with zero runtime cost.
